---
title: "1.3 Expressions and Output"
subtitle: "Using Objects and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Output

### `print` versus `println`

Java has two common output methods:

::: {.tight-list}
- `System.out.print(value)` prints without moving to a new line
- `System.out.println(value)` prints and then adds a new line
:::

A text value like `"Hi there!"` is a <span class="term">string literal</span> (字符串字面量).

## Output

### Special characters need escape sequences

Use a backslash to include special characters inside strings:

```java
System.out.println("She said \"Hi\".");
System.out.println("Line 1\nLine 2");
System.out.println("Backslash: \\");
```

The AP-safe examples to know are quotation marks, backslash, and newline.

## Arithmetic Expressions

### Operators produce one value

An <span class="term">expression</span> (表达式) combines values, variables, and operators into a result.

::: {.tight-list}
- addition `+`
- subtraction `-`
- multiplication `*`
- division `/`
:::

An expression with only `int` values evaluates to `int`. If at least one part is `double`, the result becomes `double`.

## Integer Division

### Truncating division surprises beginners

```java
System.out.println(2 / 3);   // 0
System.out.println(2.0 / 3); // 0.666...
```

When Java divides two `int` values, it uses <span class="term">truncating division</span> (截断除法): the decimal part is thrown away.

That is why `2 / 3` gives <span class="mark">`0`</span>, not `0.67` and not `1`.

## Logic Errors in Math

### Code can run and still be wrong

Not every bug is a syntax bug.

::: {.tight-list}
- `100 / 2.54` correctly converts centimeters to inches
- `100 * 2.54` compiles, runs, and gives the <span class="mark">wrong calculation</span>
:::

This is a <span class="term">logic error</span>: the program executes, but the algorithm is wrong.

## Compound Expressions

### Precedence still matters

Java follows standard precedence rules:

::: {.tight-list}
- parentheses first
- then `*`, `/`, `%`
- then `+`, `-`
:::

```java
2 + 3 * 2      // 8
(2 + 3) * 2    // 10
```

When students are unsure, extra parentheses improve readability.

## The Remainder Operator

### `%` means the leftover part

The operator <span class="term">`%`</span> returns the remainder after integer division.

::: {.tight-list}
- `5 % 2` -> `1`
- `8 % 2` -> `0`
- `3 % 4` -> `3`
:::

Important shortcut: if `x` is smaller than `y`, then `x % y` is always <span class="mark">`x`</span>.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- output prediction with `print` and `println`
- truncating-division correction tasks
- operator-precedence tracing
- <span class="term">1.3.5 Coding Challenge: Pay Calculator</span>
:::

The central teaching move is to connect <span class="mark">syntax</span>, <span class="mark">numeric type</span>, and <span class="mark">predicted output</span>.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- distinguish `print` and `println`
- define a <span class="term">string literal</span>
- evaluate arithmetic expressions with the correct operator rules
- explain why `int / int` uses truncating division
- interpret `%` as the remainder after division
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
